title: Revealing the topology of quantum states via Kirkwood-Dirac quasiprobabilities
abstract: We discuss a theoretical approach to discriminate whether two states of a many-body quantum system belong or not to different topology classes. This approach is based on expressing a strange correlator - a recently established tool for quantum topology discrimination - between the states as a function of Kirkwood-Dirac quasiprobabilities (KDQs). KDQs provide a first-principles representation of two-time quantum correlators. The link between strange correlators and KDQs allows to establish that strange correlators are weak values of an observable converting an initial trivial state into a topologically non-trivial one. We thus propose a quantum topology witness that is achievable measuring the prior and subsequent effects on a many-body system of a sudden quench transformation that realizes the transition between trivial and topological phases. The witness is evaluated on a probe quantum state whose main features are detailed within the paper. Finally, directly exploiting schemes that allows for the complete reconstruction of KDQs, we address an interferometric protocol for topology discrimination, along with a general discussion of the main lines and challenges towards its implementation.
summary: The paper introduces a novel diagnostic tool to detect the topology of many-body quantum states by analyzing 'strange correlators.' It achieves this by showing these correlators can be expressed as sums over Kirkwood-Dirac quasiprobabilities (KDQs). The ultimate goal is proposing an interferometric protocol that allows experimental measurement of these topological signatures.
main_problem: The central goal is developing a theoretical method to distinguish between different topological classes of quantum states in many-body systems.
main_result: The authors propose using 'strange correlators,' which can be mathematically decomposed into Kirkwood-Dirac quasiprobabilities (KDQs), forming a measurable quantum topology witness.
method: The approach links strange correlators to KDQs, interpreting them as weak values of an observable governing the transition between trivial and topological phases. This link is then exploited via interferometric protocols.
model_or_system: The analysis applies to general many-body quantum systems exhibiting topological order, with specific examples including the 2D Chern insulator (BHZ model) and AKLT chains. The dynamics are often analyzed through sudden quench transformations or time evolution under perturbations.
key_observables: Strange correlators ($s[\hat{o}]$), Kirkwood-Dirac quasiprobabilities ($q_{k,j}$), and the characteristic function $G(u)$ measured via quantum interferometry.
important_parameters: The scaling behavior of strange correlators with momentum/distance ($|k|^{-\alpha}$ vs $|k|^{ eta}$) is key to determining topology. The validity of linear response theory depends on time scales relative to energy gaps ($\Delta$ and $V_0$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies heavily on establishing the mathematical equivalence between strange correlators and sums over KDQs, and proposing an interferometric measurement scheme for reconstruction.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate spectral properties (BHZ model), the dependence of quasiprobability modulus $|q(kx)|$ on topological assumptions, and schematic diagrams of the quantum interferometry protocol used to measure correlation functions.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from defining strange correlators and KDQs, establishing their mathematical link, applying this framework to specific models (like BHZ), deriving linear response relations for the imaginary part of the correlator, and finally detailing an experimental/interferometric protocol for measurement.
why_interesting: This work provides a rigorous theoretical bridge between abstract topological invariants and measurable quantum correlation functions using quasiprobability formalisms, which is highly relevant for designing next-generation quantum sensing protocols.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Energy spectra of the Hamiltonian in Eq. (4) at M = 1, for periodic boundary conditions (left panel) and with open boundary conditions along y (right panel), respectively. In the right panel, gapless edge modes, topologically protected and interpolating between the two bands, are present.
Figure 4: Pictorial representation of the interferometric scheme that realizes the measurement of the real and imaginary parts of Gk(u).
